title: Product-Market Fit Research: Employer Benefit Platform
subtitle: Validating market fit for a newly acquired SaaS benefit platform across Finnish employer segments
challenge: Following the acquisition of a Finnish employer benefit SaaS platform, the company had limited insight into which market segments genuinely valued the product and why. The platform had been built for a broad 'employer' audience, but usage data suggested fragmented adoption and unclear willingness to pay. The research challenge was to rapidly validate which segments had the strongest product-market fit signals, identify key friction points blocking adoption, and provide a strategic foundation for post-acquisition positioning and roadmap priorities.

designProcess.intro: The research was structured as a rapid discovery sprint over six weeks, combining qualitative depth interviews with a quantitative pulse survey. Stakeholder alignment was treated as a phase in itself — ensuring internal hypotheses were surfaced before fieldwork began.

steps.title: Stakeholder Alignment & Hypothesis Mapping
steps.description: Ran structured interviews with the acquisition team, product owners, and sales leads to surface internal assumptions about target segments and value drivers. Synthesized these into a testable hypothesis map, distinguishing between high-confidence assumptions and genuine unknowns that fieldwork would need to resolve.

steps.title: User Interviews — HR Managers & Employees
steps.description: Conducted 47 depth interviews across two primary audiences: HR managers responsible for benefit procurement decisions, and employees as end-users of the platform. Sessions focused on current benefit management workflows, pain points, perceived value of digital tooling, and willingness-to-pay signals. Companies ranged from 50 to 2,000 employees across five industry segments.

steps.title: Survey & Synthesis
steps.description: Deployed a follow-up pulse survey to 340 HR decision-makers to validate qualitative patterns at scale. Synthesized findings into a segment opportunity matrix mapping each segment against benefit fit, adoption barriers, and revenue potential. Findings were pressure-tested in a cross-functional workshop before final recommendations were drafted.

insights: Two of five target segments showed strong PMF signals: mid-size technology companies (100–500 employees) and professional services firms, both citing admin burden reduction as the primary value driver

Willingness-to-pay was closely tied to integration capability — employers already using HRIS tools expected seamless data sync, and its absence was a hard blocker for 60% of prospects in larger segments
Employee awareness of available benefits was critically low across all segments, pointing to an adoption loop problem that the platform was not yet designed to solve
Smaller employers (<50 employees) showed interest but lacked the procurement complexity to justify the current pricing tier — a potential future segment with product simplification

images.alt: Research synthesis workshop with affinity mapping

deliverables.intro: The engagement produced three core outputs: a comprehensive research report with segment-level findings, a visual opportunity matrix for internal alignment, and a positioning recommendation memo used directly in the post-acquisition product strategy.

images.alt: Segment opportunity matrix showing PMF strength across employer verticals
Positioning recommendation framework derived from research synthesis

results.intro: The research directly shaped the post-acquisition product and go-to-market strategy, providing the company with validated segment priorities and a clear evidence base for roadmap decisions.
